name: Chachmei Lublin Yeshiva
bio: Founded in 1930 in Lublin, Poland, by Rabbi Meir Shapiro, Chachmei Lublin became a world-famous yeshiva and the birthplace of the Daf Yomi program. It was seized by the Nazis in 1939 and its library plundered. Today the restored building serves as a heritage site.

name: Finchley United Synagogue
bio: United Synagogue in Finchley, also known as Kinloss

name: Kamenitz Yeshiva
bio: Kamenitz Yeshiva was founded in 1926 in Kamenets-Litovsk by Rabbi Baruch Ber Leibowitz, a disciple of Rabbi Chaim of Brisk. It became renowned for its Brisker style of Talmudic analysis. The yeshiva flourished until it was destroyed during the Holocaust, though its traditions continued in Israel and America through its students.

name: Kelm Talmud Torah Yeshiva
bio: The Kelm Talmud Torah (often simply called 'Kelm') was a Lithuanian yeshiva established in 1866 in Kelm, Lithuania. It became one of the central institutions of the Musar Movement under Rabbi Simcha Zissel Ziv, known as the 'Saba of Kelm.' It emphasized strict order, discipline, and ethical self-mastery. The yeshiva produced many of the leading figures of the Musar and Lithuanian Torah world. It was tragically destroyed during the Holocaust in 1941 when its students and townspeople were massacred.

name: Great Synagogue of London
bio: The historic central synagogue of London, destroyed during the Blitz in 1941.

name: Mir Yeshiva
bio: Mir Yeshiva, founded in 1815 in Mir, Belarus, grew into one of the largest and most famous Lithuanian yeshivas. Under Rabbi Eliezer Yehuda Finkel and others, it became a powerhouse of Torah study. During WWII, the entire yeshiva escaped via Siberia and Japan to Shanghai, China, making it the only European yeshiva to survive intact. After the war it was reestablished in Jerusalem and Brooklyn, becoming the largest yeshiva in the world.

name: Radun Yeshiva
bio: The Radun Yeshiva was founded in 1869 in Radun, Belarus, by Rabbi Yisrael Meir Kagan, known as the Chofetz Chaim. It became famous for its emphasis on ethical conduct and halakhic study. The yeshiva was dispersed during World War I but regrouped later. It was destroyed during the Holocaust, though its legacy continued through students and the Radin Talmud Torah in Israel and the US.

name: Slabodka Yeshiva
bio: Slabodka Yeshiva, founded in the 1880s in the Slabodka suburb of Kaunas, Lithuania, was one of the most influential Lithuanian yeshivas. Under Rabbi Nosson Tzvi Finkel ('the Alter of Slabodka') it emphasized Musar, especially the idea of 'Gadlus HaAdam' (the greatness of man). It produced many leading rabbis and rosh yeshivas of the 20th century. In 1924, part of the yeshiva moved to Hebron, and later relocated to Bnei Brak after the 1929 Hebron massacre.

name: Mesivta Rabsa Eitz Chaim D'Slutsk (Slutsk-Kletsk Yeshiva)
bio: The Slutsk-Kletsk Yeshiva was an Orthodox Lithuanian yeshiva founded in 1897 in Slutsk by Rabbi Yaakov Dovid Willovsky (Ridvaz) to combat the influence of the Haskalah. It was led by Rabbi Isser Zalman Meltzer and later his son-in-law, Rabbi Aharon Kotler, who carried the yeshiva to Kletsk and ultimately reestablished it in America as Beth Medrash Govoha. The yeshiva was renowned for its elite students, intense Talmudic learning, and resilience through war, revolution, and exile.

name: Telz Yeshiva
bio: Founded in 1875 in Telšiai, Lithuania, Telz Yeshiva became a leading institution of the Mussar movement, emphasizing structured discipline alongside sharp Talmudic analysis. Led by Rabbi Eliezer Gordon and later Rabbi Yosef Leib Bloch, it trained many prominent rabbis. The original yeshiva was destroyed in the Holocaust in 1941, but a branch was reestablished in Cleveland, Ohio, in 1941 by survivors.
